segments.text:
Vaccine
A preparation of immunogenic material used to induce immunity against pathogencic organisms.

Active Immunity
Adaptive immunity that is induced by natural exposure to a pathogen or by vaccination

First Vaccines:
Variolation-China, India, 11th Century
Edward Jenner-smallpox, 1794
vacca= cow (latin)
cowpox
Poxvirus sequence similarities

Vaccinia virus used for smallpox vaccination
Vaccinia protects against Variola

Human smallpox carriers do not exist
the virus does not exist outside humans

After successful vaccination campaigns throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, the WHO certified the eradication of smallpox in 1979
Smallpox is the only human infectious disease to have been completely eradicated from nature
Success of Vaccine

End of Smallpox Vaccine Use
Routine vaccination in the US halted in 1972.
The last naturally occurring case was in 1977.
In 1978, there was an escape of smallpox from containment in a research laboratory in Birmingham, England. A medical photographer, Janet Parker died from the disease itself, after which the Professor responsible for the unit, Professor Henry Bedson, committed suicide.
The US has enough vaccine stockpiled to immunize everyone in the US.

Polio Vaccines
Grown in African green monkey kidney cells (Vero cells).
1955 Jonas Salk- Formalin Inactivated Polio Vaccine (IPV).
1958 Albert Sabin-Live attenuated virus Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV)
Cutter Incident
SV40
Success
Measles vaccine was introduced in 1962

MMR Vaccine Controversy
-case of scientific misconduct triggered a health problem
-1998 The Lancet presented apparent evidence that autism could be caused by the MMR vaccine.  The lead author of the article, Andrew Wakefield, had multiple undeclared conflicts of interest,  manipulated evidence, and had broke other ethical codes.
-The Lancet paper was partially retracted in 2004 and fully retracted in 2010, and Wakefield was found guilty by the General Medical Council of serious professional misconduct in May 2010 and was struck off the Medical Register, meaning he could no longer practice as a doctor.
-After vaccination rates dropped, the incidence of two of the three diseases increased greatly in the UK. In 1998 there were 56 confirmed cases of measles in the UK; in 2006 there were 449 in the first five months of the year, with the first death since 1992; cases occurred in inadequately vaccinated children.  Mumps cases began rising in 1999 after years of very few cases, and by 2005 the United Kingdom was in a mumps epidemic with almost 5000 notifications in the first month of 2005 alone.

Respiratory Synctial Virus
Vaccine

Maternal antibodies block effectiveness of vaccine, so many vaccines cannot be given until after they are cleared from an infants blood
(chicken pox)

A one-time injection of human rabies immune globulin (or HRIG), provides rapid, short-term protection against Rabies. Administered along with vaccine.
Rabies immune globulin is an antirabies gamma globulin obtained from the plasma of hyperimmunized human donors. It is concentrated by cold ethanol fractionation. The rabies neutralizing antibody content is usually standardized to contain 150 International Units (IU) per mL.
Injection of the vaccine proceeds on a schedule over 28 days, with a total of 5 small injections
Too many different types of cold viruses for a working vaccine

HIV-1 evolution within a single patient (33 variants)
No vaccine for HIV. The mutation rate is too high

Does it harm?      Does it help?      How does it effect
different people?

Antibodies can block viral binding to cell
Human DNA for antibody genes can generate human antibodies in mouse
Monoclonal Antibodies bind to Spike protein and block binding to ACE2 receptor
Immunization provides protection against disease
Concentration and Isotype of Serum Antibody Following Primary and Secondary Immunization with Antigen

Herd Immunity
The indirect protection from infection of susceptible members of a population, and the protection of the population as a whole, which is brought about by the presence of immune individuals
https://www.theguardian.com/society/ng-interactive/2015/feb/05/-sp-watch-how-measles-outbreak-spreads-when-kids-get-vaccinated

Real examples: Smallpox, polio, measles,

Anti-vaxers

Toxiod
A toxin that has been altered to eliminate its toxicity but that still can function as an immunogen
Cytokines make you feel bad

Conjugates
Hemophilus influenza
Tetanus toxiod

Examples of real vaccines, their origin, and characteristics
Smallpox
Chickenpox

Attenuated live vaccine against influenza  under the name FluMist.  Attenuation involved growing the virus a lower than normal temperature until a cold-adapted stain resulted.  This is unable to grow at human body temperature.  Administered intranasally , causes a transient infection of upper respiratory tract, which induces a strong immune response.

Transmission:
Smallpox is transmitted primarily through prolonged social contact or direct contact with infected body fluids or contaminated objects (fomites) such as bedding or clothes.
Infection in the natural disease is usually through  the lungs.
http://www.clinicaltrials.gov/

Reported annual number of cases in peak year in US
2004

Vaccinations are tailored for the disease
Influenza has a very short incubation time, (1-2 days) so disease symptoms are already under way by the time memory cells are activated.  Effective protection against influenza therefore depends on maintaining high levels of neutralizing antibody by repeated immunizations.  Polio incubation is more than 3 days, which gives memory cells enough time to respond.
